Data Journalism Standards

My Code of Ethics

Principles I follow when turning data into visual stories. Adapted from SPJ, Alberto Cairo, ProPublica, the AP, and 15 years of investigative reporting.

By Trevor Brown · Investigative Journalist · 2026

The Ten Principles

Principle 01

Data is evidence, not decoration

Every number in a visualization should be traceable to a primary source. If I can't cite where a data point came from, it doesn't go in the chart. I treat data with the same rigor I'd treat a source's quote in a story.

Principle 02

The chart must not lie — even by omission

A misleading axis, a cherry-picked date range, or an un-normalized map is the visual equivalent of quoting someone out of context. I design charts that show the full picture, even when the full picture is more complicated than the simple narrative.

Principle 03

Let the data lead, not the narrative

I don't start with a conclusion and find data to support it. I start with the data and follow where it goes — even when it contradicts what I expected or hoped to find. The story is in the data, not the other way around.

Principle 04

Context is not optional

A single data point without context is meaningless at best, dangerous at worst. Every chart needs baselines, comparisons, time ranges, and annotations that help the reader understand not just what the data says, but what it means.

Principle 05

Show your work

Methodology should be documented, data sources should be cited, and limitations should be disclosed — on every single graphic. If another journalist can't reproduce my analysis, I haven't been transparent enough.

Principle 06

Design for understanding, not impressiveness

A visualization that looks stunning but can't be understood in the time available to the audience is functionally misleading. On broadcast TV, that means 3-5 seconds. Clarity always wins over cleverness.

Principle 07

Say what you don't know

Every dataset has gaps, limitations, and assumptions. I state explicitly what the data cannot tell you — not just what it can. Uncertainty is information, and hiding it is dishonest.

Principle 08

Design choices are editorial choices

Scale type, color palette, map projection, chart type — each is an editorial decision that shapes how the audience perceives the data. I make these choices deliberately, justify them when asked, and default to the most honest representation.

Principle 09

Build for everyone

Accessible color palettes, clear labels, responsive layouts, and plain language. Data stories should be understandable by the broadest possible audience, not just people with technical backgrounds.

Principle 10

Correct fast, correct publicly

When I make a mistake — and I will — I fix it immediately, visibly, and without excuses. An uncorrected error in a visualization is a lie that compounds every time someone views it.

SPJ Code of Ethics — Applied to Data Viz

Seek Truth and Report It

  • Verify data before visualizing it. Use primary sources whenever possible.
  • Never deliberately distort facts or context, including visual information.
  • Provide context. Take special care not to misrepresent or oversimplify.
  • Neither speed nor format excuses inaccuracy.

Minimize Harm

  • Consider whether visualizations could be used to target or stereotype communities.
  • Balance the public's need for information against potential harm.
  • When mapping demographic data, consider privacy implications.

Act Independently

  • Don't let the desired narrative drive chart design choices.
  • Choose chart types based on what best represents the data, not what supports a thesis.
  • Disclose any data limitations that could affect interpretation.

Be Accountable and Transparent

  • Show your work. Include methodology, sources, and limitations.
  • Respond to questions about accuracy, clarity, and fairness.
  • Acknowledge and correct mistakes promptly.

Alberto Cairo's Five Qualities

From The Truthful Art — every visualization I build should be:

  1. Truthful — Based on thorough research and honest data
  2. Functional — Accomplishes its communicative purpose clearly
  3. Beautiful — Aesthetically purposeful, not decorative
  4. Insightful — Reveals something the raw numbers alone don't show
  5. Enlightening — Combines all four to create genuine understanding

D3.js-Specific Standards

D3 gives total control over every pixel. That means total responsibility.

Scales

  • Bar charts: always start the y-axis at zero.
  • Linear vs. log vs. sqrt — each tells a different story. Justify the choice.
  • Never stretch aspect ratios to exaggerate trends.

Maps

  • Map projections are editorial decisions. Document which projection and why.
  • Choropleths must use normalized data (per capita, percentages) — never raw counts.
  • Color classification method (equal intervals, quantiles, natural breaks) affects what patterns the reader sees.

Color

  • Use colorblind-safe palettes. Avoid pure red-green contrasts.
  • Be intentional about political color associations.
  • Ensure differences are distinguishable in grayscale.

Animation

  • Use to aid comprehension, never to dazzle.
  • Don't imply continuity or causation where none exists.
  • For broadcast: if it can't be understood in 3-5 seconds, simplify.

Election & Political Data Standards

Given my focus on voting rights, elections, and public policy data, I hold myself to these additional standards:

  • Frame carefully. Use "reliance on" and "exposure to" — not overclaims about what "will" happen.
  • Show uncertainty. Polling data and projections are probabilistic, not deterministic.
  • Full time series. Always show the complete available range; if zooming in, provide the broader context alongside.
  • Distinguish precisely between "registered voters," "votes cast," and "eligible voters."
  • Normalize everything. Raw vote counts on a map are almost always misleading.
  • Geographic fairness. Land area is not population. Consider cartograms or proportional approaches for election data.

Pre-Publication Checklist

  1. Is every data point traceable to a cited primary source?
  2. Does the y-axis start at zero on bar charts?
  3. Is map data normalized (per capita/percentage)?
  4. Are colors accessible for colorblind viewers?
  5. Is the time range complete and not cherry-picked?
  6. Are limitations and methodology documented?
  7. Would a non-expert correctly understand this in under 10 seconds?
  8. Does the chart look the same if I swap my assumptions?
  9. Have I shown this to someone unfamiliar with the topic?
  10. Would I stand behind every design choice if questioned on air?

Influences & References

  • SPJ Code of Ethics — Society of Professional Journalists
  • The Truthful Art & How Charts Lie — Alberto Cairo
  • ProPublica Code of Ethics — ProPublica
  • AP Stylebook — Data Journalism chapter (2017+)
  • IRE / NICAR — Investigative Reporters and Editors
  • Sigma Awards — Global data journalism standards
  • Fundamentals of Data Visualization — Claus Wilke
  • 15 years of investigative reporting — the instincts carry over
Trevor Brown
Investigative Journalist & Data Visualization Developer
Last updated: March 2026
